title: Why your skin crawls when someone violates your boundaries
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
It's not just in your head. It's in your skin. 

 You know that feeling. Someone crosses a line. Maybe they touch you without asking. Maybe they interrupt you...
metaDescription:
It's not just in your head. It's in your skin. 

 You know that feeling. Someone crosses a line. Maybe they touch you without asking. Maybe they interrupt...
htmlContent:
It's not just in your head. It's in your skin.

You know that feeling. Someone crosses a line. Maybe they touch you without asking. Maybe they interrupt you for the fifth time. Maybe they push you to share something you're not ready to share.

And suddenly, your skin crawls.

It's not a metaphor. It's not an exaggeration. Your body is actually having a physical reaction. Your stomach might clench. Your shoulders might tighten. You might feel a wave of heat or a sudden chill. You might feel like you need to shower, even though nothing "happened" in the way other people would describe as a violation.

Here's the thing. That crawling sensation is your nervous system talking to you. And it's not being dramatic. It's being precise.

I've felt this so many times. For years, I thought something was wrong with me. I thought I was too sensitive. Too fragile. Too broken. I would feel that skin-crawling sensation and immediately tell myself to calm down, to stop overreacting, to be more reasonable.

But here's what I've learned. That sensation is a signal. And when we ignore it, we pay a price.

What your nervous system is actually doing

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that the crawling sensation is your dorsal vagal system activating. That's the part of your nervous system that handles immobilization, shutdown, and protection. It's the oldest part of our evolutionary response system.

Think about it. When an animal in the wild senses danger, it has three options. Fight, flight, or freeze. But freeze isn't just about playing dead. It's also about dissociation. It's about numbing out. It's about making the body feel less present so the pain is more bearable.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

That crawling sensation? It's your body's way of saying "I need to get out of this situation, but I'm not sure I can." It's a pre-freeze signal. It's your nervous system preparing to protect you by disconnecting you from your body.

Peter Levine, who wrote extensively about somatic experiencing, calls this the body's "felt sense." And he's clear that our bodies know things our conscious minds don't. That crawling sensation is information. It's data. It's a message from a part of you that has been paying attention to threats for your entire life.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine walks through exactly how the body stores trauma and how we can release it. It's not an easy read. But it's a clarifying one.

Here's what I want you to understand. That crawling feeling isn't a sign that you're weak. It's a sign that your body is working. It's doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's trying to protect you.

The cost of ignoring the signal

I spent years ignoring that signal. Someone would violate a boundary, and I'd feel that crawl. But I'd override it. I'd smile. I'd say "it's fine." I'd tell myself I was being too sensitive.

And then I'd wonder why I felt exhausted. Why I had headaches. Why my back hurt. Why I couldn't sleep.

Gabor Mate, a physician who has spent decades studying the connection between stress and illness, would not be surprised. He's written extensively about how suppressed emotions and ignored boundaries create physical disease. His work with patients shows that chronic illness often has roots in chronic boundary violations.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Mate documents case after case where people who couldn't say "no" to others ended up with bodies that said "no" for them. Autoimmune conditions. Chronic pain. Cancer. The pattern is striking.

When you ignore the crawling sensation, you're telling your body that its signals don't matter. You're telling it that your social obligations are more important than its wisdom. And your body remembers that.

Your body remembers what your mind has forgotten. That's not poetry - that's neuroscience. Peter Levine spent decades documenting how trauma lives in the tissues, in the fascia, in the way your muscles hold tension patterns that mirror the exact posture you were in when the worst thing happened.

I've seen this in my own body. The way my shoulders round forward when I feel threatened - that's the fetal position, compressed into a standing posture. The way my jaw clenches before I even know I'm stressed - that's years of swallowed words, swallowed screams, swallowed truth. Your body is an archive. It stores everything.

Here's what I want you to try. Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Don't try to change anything. Just notice. Is your breath high and shallow? Is your belly tight? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Whatever you find, it's okay. You're not doing it wrong. You're just meeting yourself where you are. That's the beginning of everything.

Your body keeps score. That's not a metaphor either. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote the seminal book on trauma, has spent his career showing how trauma lives in the body. Not in memories you can talk about. In physical sensations, in tension patterns, in chronic activation.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk walks through the research on how trauma reshapes the brain and the body. It's dense. But it's worth it.

Every time you override the crawl, you're adding to the score. You're teaching your body that its signals are not trustworthy. You're building a pattern of self-betrayal that has real physical consequences.

Learning to listen instead of override

So what do we do with this? How do we stop ignoring the signal and start honoring it?

First, we have to slow down. That crawling sensation usually lasts for a few seconds before we override it. If we can catch it in that window, we have a choice. We can notice it. We can name it. We can say to ourselves, "Oh, my body is telling me something."

Second, we have to trust it. This is the hard part. Because we've been trained our whole lives to distrust our bodies. We've been told that our feelings are wrong, that we're overreacting, that we need to be more reasonable. But your body doesn't lie. It might be overprotective. It might be responding to old wounds. But it's not lying.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying you should act on every sensation. If your body screams "danger" when someone looks at you a certain way, that might be a trauma response. You don't have to run out of the room. But you also don't have to pretend it's not happening. You can acknowledge it. You can say to yourself, "My body is having a reaction. That's real. That's valid. Let me stay present with it."

Third, we have to practice boundary-setting in safe environments. You can't learn to trust your body's signals in the middle of a crisis. You have to practice when the stakes are low. Say no to something small. Express a preference. Ask for what you need. Notice how your body feels when you do. Notice how it feels when you don't.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get enough attention. The way chronic stress changes your relationship with pain. When your nervous system has been in overdrive for years, your pain threshold shifts. Things that shouldn't hurt, hurt. Things that should hurt, don't register at all. Your body's volume knob is broken.

This is what the research on central sensitization shows. The spinal cord and brain start amplifying pain signals. Fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, irritable bowel - these aren't imaginary. They're the body's honest response to a nervous system that never got to complete its stress cycle. The energy that was meant for fight or flight got trapped. And trapped energy has to go somewhere.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've been told your pain isn't real, if you've been dismissed by doctors, if you've started to doubt your own body - I want you to know something. Your body isn't lying. It's speaking the only language it has. The question isn't whether the pain is real. The question is what your body is trying to tell you through it.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). It's not a solution to boundary violations. But it can help your nervous system regulate after the fact. It can help you feel more grounded in your body, which makes it easier to listen to those signals next time.

Wild, right? That something as simple as a blanket can help you reconnect with your body's wisdom. But that's how this works. Small steps. Consistent practice. Building trust slowly.

The grief of what we've ignored

Here's the part nobody talks about. When you start listening to that crawling sensation, you're going to feel a lot of grief. Because you're going to realize how many times you ignored it. How many times you betrayed yourself. How many times you stayed in situations that made your skin crawl because you didn't feel like you had a choice.

That grief is real. It's valid. And it's part of the healing process.

You might feel anger too. Anger at the people who crossed your boundaries. Anger at the culture that told you to be nice. Anger at yourself for not listening sooner.

Let yourself feel it. Don't rush past it. Don't try to "positive thinking" your way out of it. The grief and anger are signals too. They're telling you that you know what you deserve. They're telling you that you're ready for something different.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tense up when I said that? Did you feel a wave of sadness? Did you want to skip this section and move to the next one?

That's your nervous system trying to protect you from painful feelings. And I get it. I do the same thing. But here's the truth. The only way out is through. The only way to stop feeling the crawl is to actually listen to it. To let it tell you what it needs to tell you. To let the grief move through you.

Your body has been carrying this for a long time. It deserves to be heard.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's look at how trauma reshapes the brain and body.
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's guide to somatic experiencing and releasing trauma stored in the body.
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate's exploration of the link between stress, suppressed emotions, and chronic illness.
  • YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link) - A grounding tool to help regulate your nervous system and feel more present in your body.
